entry.title: Re: Designing Product Hierarchy in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello!

A Product Hierarchy ID can be assigned to multiple distribution chains (Sales Org/Distribution Channel combos). Each distribution chain/purpose combo can only have one active (non-overlapping) assignment at a time, that's causing  "the key value is already in use" in screenshot.

Once a hierarchy timeframe has been used via an assignment: you can move or delete existing nodes, you can add new nodes, you can add new subnodes directly under the root node. But you can't delete or move existing subnodes, and you can't deactivate or delete the hierarchy timeframe even if you delete the assignment later.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/6507e7b8f29944ed9bc72cf2b04b5c7f.html?locale=en-US

Product Hierarchy in S/4HANA Cloud is a time-dependent, versioned object by design at the hierarchy ID level itself, separate from the assignment. A hierarchy version can be copied to a new version for a different time period under the same product hierarchy ID, or as a completely new product hierarchy with a different Hierarchy ID. The recommended pattern is:

  • Keep the Hierarchy ID stable as the actual business classification (e.g. A002, TOOLS), this is what's referenced in pricing conditions, reporting, and other downstream consumption, so churning the ID itself breaks continuity there.
  • When is needed structural changes beyond what's allowed on the active version (deleting/moving existing subnodes), create a new time-dependent version of the same Hierarchy ID with its own Valid From date via "Manage Product Hierarchies" (copy → new version → edit structure → activate). While creating a product hierarchy, you enter Hierarchy ID, description and a Valid From date, this version is valid on activation from Valid From until Valid To, and you can adjust Valid To for a time-dependent hierarchy rather than leaving it open-ended. https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/introduction-to-product-hierarchy-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/12864494
  • In Manage Product Hierarchy Assignments, close out the old assignment's Valid To date (use "Change Validity") to match where the old hierarchy version ends, and create the new assignment for the same distribution chain starting the day the new version becomes valid.

 

To fix the error: cancel this create, go back to the A002/Denmark/BU row, use Change Validity to set its Valid To to the day before your intended cutover (e.g. 2025/12/31), then create the A003 assignment starting 2026/01/01, same pattern already used for the IC channel rows in your list.

entry.title: Re: Inquiry on Depreciation Key Configuration to Comply w...
entry.content:
Hello!

There is no single standard depreciation key or period control that reproduces this exact rule (day-based proration in the acquisition month only, followed by fixed equal monthly amounts thereafter). Period control methods only prorate at the period level, options like "pro rata at period start date," "pro rata up to mid-period at period start date," half-month conventions, etc. Depreciation key "SUL2" for example uses an 'Acq' setting of 02, "pro rata upto mid-period at period start date", but none of the delivered period controls calculate proration based on the exact number of calendar days in the first month.

"Depreciation to the Day" does calculate to the exact day, but it does this for the entire useful life, not just the acquisition month. This means every period's depreciation amount would fluctuate slightly with the actual number of days in that period, rather than settling into a fixed monthly amount from month 2 onward as Circular 45 requires.

A similar question was raised on SAP Community, the requester noted that depreciation-to-the-day can't be used because it computes daily depreciation for the entire useful life, and period control methods can't be used because they all prorate by period, not by exact days. Recommended a manual workaround (below):

  • to split the asset's depreciation into two time intervals (phases) in the multi-level method:

Phase 1:  covers only the acquisition month. Since standard calculation methods can't derive the exact prorated amount automatically, this phase's amount is determined manually: you calculate the day-prorated first-month figure outside the system and post it as the planned depreciation for that period.

Phase 2: starts the following period, using a standard straight-line depreciation key with period control "pro rata at period start date" (or equivalent), which then creatrs fixed monthly amount (Annual/12) automatically for the rest of the useful life.

This requires manual intervention for each asset's first-month figure, but avoids ongoing manual work for all subsequent periods.

 

References:

SAP Community: Asset depreciation starting from capitalization date for Vietnam Localization https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/asset-depreciation-starting-from-capitalization-date-for-vietnam/qaq-p/12773818

SAP Community: Depreciation to the Day Functionality

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/depreciation-to-the-day-functionality/ba-p/13437948

SAP Community: Asset Accounting - Configuration Guide for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition (covers the Expert Configuration Service / Depreciation Key Template process). https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/asset-accounting-configuration-guide-for-sap-s-4hana-public-cloud-edition/ba-p/14134075

SAP KBA 3480268 - Depreciation to the day and period control methods (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

SAP KBA 2965145 - How to depreciate asset capitalized after mid-period on the first day of next month

SAP KBA 3220479 - Depreciation starts in wrong period (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

entry.title: Concurrency lock on TM transport order during mass approval from the 'My Inbox' Fiori app.
entry.content:
I am currently working on an approval workflow that processes records entered in the 'Transport Charges' node; this triggers a workflow that sends each entered value individually.
This means that each value has its own workitem and its own container.

The issue arises when a user selects multiple lines belonging to the same freight order in the Fiori 'My Inbox' app and performs a mass approval or rejection. Fiori begins processing them one by one; the first work item processes correctly but places a lock of type '/BOBF/S_LIB_ENQUEUE_NODE' on the freight order.
Consequently, when the second value attempts the same action, it finds the object locked by the first work item a pattern that repeats for all subsequent work items attempting to modify the same freight order.

  This is the problematic code snippet:

    IF lt_mod IS NOT INITIAL.
      lo_srv_mgr->modify(
        EXPORTING it_modification = lt_mod
        IMPORTING eo_change       = DATA(lo_change)
                  eo_message      = lo_message ).

      IF lo_change->has_failed_changes( ) = abap_false.
        lo_srv_mgr->do_action(
          EXPORTING
            iv_act_key           = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_action-root-calc_transportation_charges
            it_key               = lt_tor_root_key
          IMPORTING
            eo_change            = DATA(lo_act_change)
            eo_message           = DATA(lo_act_msg) ).

        IF lo_act_change IS BOUND AND lo_act_change->has_failed_changes( ) = abap_false.
          lo_tra->save( IMPORTING eo_message = lo_message ).
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF. " End lt_mod

I have already tried removing the 'optimistic' and 'exclusive' locks on the lock object '/BOBF/E_LIB_2' after processing was complete, allowing a couple of seconds to elapse using the function module 'DEQUEUE_/BOBF/E_LIB_2', but without success.
I would appreciate your help if any of you have encountered a similar scenario.

Thanks, and I hope you are all doing well.

entry.title: Re: Cannot use Country Group in Manage Allocation
entry.content:
Good day @chonpisit 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, the most likely reason is that you created your hierarchy in Manage Global Hierarchies, but it was not made available as a compatible group for use in allocation fields, or the validity dates do not match the allocation cycle.

In simple terms:

  • A hierarchy created in Manage Global Hierarchies is not automatically available as a group in all consuming apps.
  • To use it as a group, note that you must select Use in Compatible hierarchy type Group when editing the hierarchy. For your case, this should be the compatible Country/Region Group setting.
  • The hierarchy must also be active and valid for the same period as the allocation cycle. KBA 3675383 describes the same symptom for WBS element groups in Manage Allocations and states that the cycle validity must not start before the hierarchy validity or end after the hierarchy validity.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8e040a45e4db4e6abd426bee0a86dc8a.html?locale=en-US

 

I suggest you check the following:

In the Manage Global Hierarchies app, open your hierarchy, choose edit

Check that the hierarchy type is Country/Region, select Use in Compatible Country/Region Group, if available, check the Valid Form and Valid To dates and activate the hierarchy again.

 

Make sure you check the allocation cycle as follows:

In Manage Allocations, open the allocation cycle and segments, check the cycle Valid From and Valid To dates. Make sure your cycle validity is fully inside the country hierarchy validity period.

Then go to the Receiver tab, Use receiver object Country, ensure your value type is set to Group.   

You should search with the hierarchy ID, not only the description.

After you have concluded these steps, the country group should become available if the hierarchy type, compatible group flag, activation status, and validity dates are correct.

 

If the checkbox Use in Compatible Country/Region Group is not available for this hierarchy type in your tenant, or the group is still not available for you after these checks, then you probably have a limitation or enablement issue for that allocation context. In that case, you should make use of individual country values as the receiver selection or raise a case with SAP.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Joule in SAP PaPM UM Cloud: Light or Intensive - Choose Right Before You Switch It On
entry.content:
AI gets all its applause for what it can do.

Answer questions, explain an untraceable number, guide a user through a process, and remove an hour of manual work.

But none of that happens in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud the moment Joule shows up on the screen. It happens when one quieter thing is true first: the connection is set up correctly.

Here’s the part most teams skip past - there isn’t one way to connect Joule. There are two. And picking the wrong one for how you actually plan to use AI is the whole difference between “it just works” and “why is it so slow, why did that fail, why can’t I get in?”

So before you switch anything on, answer one honest question: Are we using AI lightly, or are we about to lean on it every single day?

Let’s walk both paths.

Path 1: Light Usage - It’s Already In the Box
Here’s the good news that saves a lot of effort: SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud already ships with built-in AI, ready to use through a preinstalled Joule client. No dedicated AI Core, no model to deploy, no destination to wire. It’s already there - you don’t build the connection, you just start asking.

There’s one rule you have to respect, and it’s there by design. A Fair Use Policy applies per day, per tenant. Stay inside light usage and everything runs smoothly. Push past it - too many requests, too fast - and automatic throttling steps in to protect the shared capacity.

Think of it as a shared lane on a highway. Perfectly quick when traffic is normal, but it isn’t your private road. Flood it, and the system deliberately slows you to keep things fair for everyone else on the tenant. That single fact is what makes light usage perfect for occasional AI - and the wrong fit for heavy, all-day work.

To be clear, light usage isn't a preview - it's the full experience. Here are some assistants you'll find across the product:

  1. Report and Simulation Assistant: Adjust a what-if scenario inside a report (set the What-If percentage for Account Training to 90 and watch the plan-and-forecast update).
  2. Process and Activities Assistant: Drive processes by conversation-activate a process for an environment, confirm it's live, and see how many activities it carries.
  3. Analysis Assistant (in the Show screen): Ask plain-language questions about the data ("what's the total for this GL account this fiscal year?") and get precise, context-aware answers.
  4. Model Assistant: Build your model by describing it - create environments, functions, fields, and more, without clicking through every screen.
  5. Knowledge Assistant: Ask how things work and get answers grounded in product knowledge, so guidance is right where you're working.
  6. Connection Assistant: Set up and check your connections conversationally, so data sources and integrations come together with less manual effort.

These are a few examples: Joule appears in more places, and its capabilities keep expanding.

If you use light, the entire “setup” comes down to three things:

  1. Confirm your subscription is active and that the Joule client is available.
  2. Ensure that you are assigned the required roles.
  3. Enter your request in Joule and proceed with your task.

If that's you, you can stop reading here - you're done, and you didn't build a thing.

Path 2: Intensive Usage - Bring Your Own Engine

But what if AI isn’t an occasional helper? What if it’s becoming part of daily operations - many users, many questions, all day? Then the shared lane stops being enough, and the guidance is blunt about it: for intensive usage, set up and connect to a dedicated SAP AI Core to ensure high speed. This is where you stop borrowing capacity and start running your own engine - more headroom, better performance, more control.

Heads-up on regions. Create and use your AI Core instance in the same region as your current PaPM instance - and only where AI Core is actually supported. The supported regions today are ap10, ap11, ap20, ap30, br10, eu10, eu11, eu20, eu30, in30 jp10, us10, us21, and us30. Confirm your PaPM region is on this list before you start.

Once it’s in place, the rest is one clean flow through four stages - and the order is the whole game:

 

① Build the engine - SAP AI Core

The first stage stands up the engine that actually runs the models, and it ends with the single most important artifact in this whole process: a service key. Make sure you’re on the AI Core Extended plan here - that’s the one that unlocks generative AI.

Here’s how to set it up:

  1. Create a subaccount in SAP BTP (You can use a different subaccount from the one used for SAP Profitability and Performance Management.)
  2. Enable the Cloud Foundry environment in the subaccount.
  3. Create a space within the Cloud Foundry environment.
  4. Add the required service plan for SAP AI Core.
  5. Create a service instance of SAP AI Core.
  6. Create and download the service key for the service instance.

Treat that key like the crown jewels. The URL, client ID, and client secret inside it are exactly what you’ll paste into the destination two stages from now. Everything downstream reads from this one file - save it somewhere safe and don’t lose it.

See detailed setup steps : Build the engine - SAP AI Core 

② Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad

A model you’re entitled to isn’t automatically up and running. Stage tow closes that gap. Start by choosing the AI Launchpad Standard plan, then set it up just like you did with SAP AI Core - create a subaccount, set up Cloud Foundry, and add the service plan. From there, subscribe to the service, assign yourself the necessary access, and launch it.

Now the real work, and it's a short sequence. First, add a connection to SAP AI Core using the service key from Stage ①. Then create a configuration: give it a name, choose the foundation-models scenario, and pick the model you want to serve.

From that configuration, create a deployment - the live endpoint that will actually answer requests.

If this is your very first Launchpad, you'll also need to create the supported embedding model, text-embedding-ada-002 - the integration depends on it.

And then you wait for one word. Open the deployment and watch its status until it reads “Running.” Need more than one model? Just repeat the configure-and-deploy loop for each.

This is the gate for everything that follows. If the deployment isn’t “Running,” the destination you build next has nothing on the other end - and Joule will look connected while every question quietly vanishes.

See detailed setup steps : Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad 

③ Build the bridge - the BTP destination

With a running model, stage three connects PaPM to it through an HTTP destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit. You’ll head into your PaPM subaccount, open Connectivity → Destinations, and create a new one from scratch.

This is the one place where the exact values matter, because a typo here is invisible until Joule silently fails - so slow down. And here’s the detail you can’t get wrong: the name is not free form. For Joule in the Universal Model, the destination must use the predefined name PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL. Get a single character wrong and the integration won’t find it.

With the name set, the rest is transcription. Set the type to HTTP, proxy type to Internet, and authentication to OAuth2ClientCredentials. The URL is your service key’s API URL with /v2/inference on the end; the Client ID and Client Secret come straight out of that same service key; and the Token Service URL is the key’s URL with /oauth/token appended, using a Dedicated token service type. Leave the two mTLS / basic-credentials checkboxes unticked.

Then comes the part that actually decides how Joule behaves - the Additional properties. This is where you choose the models, so it’s worth getting right. There are four:

configuration Parameters

 
Parameter

Requirement

Description

ai-resource-group

Required

Your AI Launchpad workspace’s resource group. Often just default.

ai_modelRequiredThe primary model Joule reasons with. Recommended: anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet, claude-4.6-opus, claude-4.5-sonnet, or claude-4.5-opus.
websearch_modelOptionalSet it to sonar or sonar-pro to let Joule pull real-time web search into its answers.
ai_support_modelOptionalA lighter secondary model (anthropic--claude-4.5-haiku or gemini-2.5-flash) that handles the simpler tasks alongside the primary one.
 

Fill those in, then save.

Every one of those service-key values is sitting in the file you downloaded in Stage ①. This stage is really just transcribing that key carefully into the right boxes - under the one name the integration expects - and choosing which models do the thinking.

See detailed setup steps : Build the bridge - the BTP destination 

④ It’s already live - tenant-wide

Here’s the quietly satisfying part: there’s no final “now point PaPM at it” step to fumble. Because the system finds the connection by its fixed name, the moment you save PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL, it applies across every environment in the tenant. No per-environment wiring, no name to copy into a settings screen, nothing left to forget.

That’s the whole handshake. The engine is running, the model is deployed and “Running,” and the destination is in place under the name Joule’s Universal Agent goes looking for. Joule goes live - only now it’s drawing on your own dedicated AI Core. Same assistant your users see; far more room to breathe behind it.

A Word on Capacity - Size It Before You Sign
That €10k minimum buys Capacity Units, and how far they stretch depends entirely on which model you run and how hard you run it.

The lesson hiding in that trade-off is simple - capacity is a function of the model. A chatty model with long answers burns far more than a lean one. So don’t guess. SAP’s Generative AI hub Cost Calculator lets you plug in your model, your token sizes, and your monthly volume and tells you what you’ll actually consume. Run that before you finalize the BTPEA, not after the invoice surprises you.

How to Choose - In One Breath

If your AI use is occasional, stay light: it’s already built in, you respect the daily Fair Use Policy, you accept that heavy bursts get throttled, and you start today without building anything.

If AI is becoming part of daily business, go intensive: confirm your region is supported, get the BTPEA in place, then walk the four stages in order - engine, model, bridge, live - naming the destination exactly PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL and choosing your models in the destination’s properties, and size your Capacity Units against the model you actually intend to use.

The real mistake isn’t choosing one over the other. It’s not choosing - drifting into heavy use on the light model and wondering why it throttles, or paying for a dedicated engine you don’t yet need.

Final Thought

The best AI experiences feel effortless to the person using them. They almost never get that way by accident.

Behind a smooth Joule moment in Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud, there’s a decision someone made on purpose: light or intensive, fair-use lane or dedicated engine, the right model sized to the real workload - and, when it’s intensive, four stages walked in the right order.

Get the quiet groundwork right, and Joule stops being a button in the corner of the screen. It becomes the assistant you actually reach for.

For the complete, official step-by-step instructions, see the SAP Help Portal 

To understand Joule capability, see the Introducing the Joule in Universal Model for SAP PaPM: When Your Model Finally Talks Back..

entry.title: Hello from India - A Fintech Risk Professional now stepping into the SAP world!
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Prateek Kumar and I'm joining this community as a complete newcomer to SAP - but not a newcomer to
the world of risk and compliance.

A little about my journey so far:

I've spent 7+ years working in the Indian fintech and payments industry across companies like One97 Communications & Cashfree Payments, handling KYC/AML compliance, fraud monitoring, merchant risk management, and RBI regulatory frameworks. I've led teams of 30+ people in compliance and risk operations - essentially building and  running the kind of controls that SAP GRC automates.

That realization hit me recently - everything I've been doing manually (Segregation of Duties, access risk analysis, compliance frameworks, audit trails) has a structured SAP equivalent. So here I am, making a deliberate career pivot into SAP GRC consulting.

I hold a B.Tech in Computer Science, so the technical side doesn't scare me - but I'm genuinely starting from scratch on SAP and have a lot to learn.

My goal is to transition into a GRC consulting role by end of 2026 and I'm currently studying every day toward that target.

I'm really looking forward to learning from this incredible community. If you've made a similar jump from a domain/compliance background into SAP - I'd especially love to hear your story and connect!

Happy to be here.

Prateek Kumar
Senior Manager | Fintech & Payments | India
Transitioning to → SAP GRC Consulting

entry.title: Re: Maintain Authorization Group Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @DKN1 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, you cannot handle it in the same way as on premise CT04/PFCG customizing. In your cloud authorization model, access is mainly controlled by business roles, business catalogs define access to app and the restrictions redefine access for business roles.

So, in simple terms:

  • You should not look for the classic on premise customizing where authorization objects are assigned manually to an authorization group.
  • In Mange Characteristics (CT04), you should check first whether the required access can be controlled through the relevant business role and available restrictions.
  • If your Authorization Group is visible in the characteristics but your value help is empty, i would not assume that you can freely create new values unless it provides a released configuration for that exact object.

See the following suggested reviews for more details

In Maintain Business Roles, check the business role assigned to the user who maintains characteristics and open Manage Characteristics (CT04) and display the affected characteristics.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/8980ad05330b4585ab96a8e09cef4688.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/s4hana-best-practices/create-batch-specification-master-data-2zw-c80b058252c363d87984fd7b9cfeb6e7/create-characteristic?locale=en-US

If your requirements is for aATP product allocation characteristics value access, you should use Manage Characteristics Catalogs, not CT04.

If your requirements is for another area, for example Quality Management master data, you should check the specific application area.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/f5dd25db7d9346cd8191b9191cf7fe72.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/6ee6a6ae1ded4e47aaaff79ccd357428.html?locale=en-US  

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
    Waiting; // Session created, not yet started
    Playing; // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
    Moving; // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
    Blocked; // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
    Finished; // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
    key square       : Integer; // 1–100
        snakeTo      : Integer; // 0 if no snake on this square
        ladderTo     : Integer; // 0 if no ladder on this square
        isDoubleHead : Boolean default false; // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
    key ID         : UUID;
        name       : String(100); // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
        createdAt  : Timestamp;
        startedAt  : Timestamp;
        finishedAt : Timestamp;
        winner     : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
    key ID         : UUID;
        session    : Association to GameSessions;
        name       : String(100);

        @readonly
        position   : Integer default 1; // current square (1–100)

        @readonly
        TurnStatus : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
        turnOrder  : Integer; // 1 = goes first
        lastRoll   : Integer; // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using {snakeladder} from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

    entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

    @flow.status: TurnStatus
    entity Players      as projection on snakeladder.Players
        actions {

            // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
            @from: [ #Waiting]
            @TO  : #Playing
            action startTurn();

            @from: [ #Playing]
            @TO  : #Moving
            action rollDice() returns Integer;

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Waiting // transitions to next player in Week 3
            action confirmMove();

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Blocked // double-headed snake bite
            action blockPlayer();

            @from: [ #Blocked]
            @TO  : $flow.previous // restores pre-block status
            action unblockPlayer();

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Finished
            action winGame();
        }

    entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 

I decided to keep the same TurnStatus to try with default lifecycle steps for now

entry.title: Re: Released CDS Views for Handling Unit Information in I...
entry.content:
Hello @ashoktirumalasetti30 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your scenario, the combined CDS views you found should not be used if they are not released for extensibility use case. See KBA 3504675 for more details.

So, in simple terms:

  • I HandlingUnitHeaderCombined and I HandlingUnitItemCombined may contain the data, but they are not the right objects for your case if they are not released.
  • Instead, make sure you use the released Handling Unit CDS views first.
  • If the HU is warehouse managed, also check the warehouse specific HU CDS/API options.

I suggest you check I HandlingUnitHeader and I HandlingUnitItem in your system.

However, note that the Handling Unit Header is valid for handling units in storage locations managed in Logistics General, not for handling units in storage locations managed with Warehouse Management. If your HU is warehouse managed, check Warehouse Handling Unit Header instead for more insights.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/f965bf9b58dd4635bc9a4de7cae1ff11.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9ce855c709ce4f5c99b685903461106e/0831a7112057480a8f61b8bf01b56202.html?locale=en-US

For API based access, OData API is provided for the Handling Unit. Note that the service can retrieve, create, and delete handling units and post goods movements for handling units. Be aware that the required business catalog for calling this service is SAP_LO_BC_HNDLGUNIT_PC.

For hierarchy or top-level HU logic, consult KBA 3721632 for more insights.

Here is a quick check you should consider:

In View Browser, search for I_HandlingUnitHeader, I_HandlingUnitItem, and Warehouse Handling Unit Header.  

Check Key User Release State and Cloud Dev. Release State. See KBA 3504675 on the requirements.

Take note of the CDS view name, release state, and fields required for HU number, delivery reference, product, quantity and packaging material

If CDS views do not cover your need, test the Handling Unit OData API with the required communication user role.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 2LIS_03_BX and 2LIS_03_BF equivalent CDS views in S/4
entry.content:
Hi,

Using CDS views isn't a hard mandate today, and there is no direct 1:1 equivalent CDS view in SAP S/4HANA. — SAP Note 2500202 still classifies 2LIS_03_BX, 2LIS_03_BF, and 2LIS_03_UM as working extractors in S/4HANA (via compatibility views over the new MATDOC-based inventory model), and we've already validated this ourselves through our own conversion testing, including custom fields on 2LIS_03_BF.

That said, CDS/ODP_CDS is SAP's strategic direction, and there's no 1:1 SAP-delivered CDS replacement for BX/BF/UM today

keep the current extractors for now (lowest risk, already proven), monitor Note 2499310 for any future deprecation signal on inventory,

entry.title: Re: Enable barcode scanning for Product field (/SCWM/S_RF...
entry.content:
Hello @KGANTI2022 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, you should not enhance the standard RF Product field/SCWM/S_RF_SELECTTION- RFPROD for a long composite GS1/EAN128 string because, the RF Stock/Bin Query by Product flow allows stock and bin queries product and product attributes using RF device. You should scan one long composite string into a field that is technically the Product input field. If you notice that the field is limited and has a product conversion logic, truncations before barcode parsing is expected from a technical design perspective and during that flow, the product field is not generic barcode payload field that can first decode the product and batch and then populate both fields.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/4ba1d099972344159e660ca9976d070e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/735bae8c309246a79e0e926c2374d1f0.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/e9c79a81dffa48c2b9d76f41875e1cb7.html?locale=en-US

Here are some options:

Option 1: You should use a standard GS1 scanning only where the RF transactions support the composite barcode logic. You should test the same GS1/EAN128 barcode in the relevant RF process where the product and batch scanning is totally supported.

Option 2: For your specific RF query, make sure you only pass the product value into the Product field, then enter or scan the batch separately where the screen supports it.

Option 3: You should not build a modified standard RF screen in the core. In Public Cloud, custom development must use released APIs, BAdIs, released objects, and predefined extension points only. Therefore, I wound not recommend a custom replacement of the standard RF program or changing the standard field exit unless a released extension point for this exact RF transaction is provided.

If your business process requires one scan to split product and batch in this specific query transaction, I suggest you rais a case with SAP and provide the necessary details.

 

See the following references for more details

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/using-developer-in-app-extensibility-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition?

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.content:
Hello Zhende,

The History tables (EBEWH, MBEWH...) are updated with first movement after a period closure.

If there is no movement for several months, then you will not see any history record for all those months that have past.

So you can check the the posting to this material in 2024, or the data in 2024 have been migrated or transported.

Hope this help!

entry.title: Re: Fiori Reports for Reviewing Preliminary Actual Costin...

Hello Vijay,

As far as I know SAP does not provide a standard report specifically designed as a “pre-posting Actual Costing report” before the Post Closing step. The primary analysis is performed within the Actual Costing run itself.

You can check CKM3N for analyze preliminary value, CCS, at material/ plant level. But CKM3N can only display single material, therefore you can create your own drilldown report KKML0.

Hope this help!

entry.title: How to Implement a Queuing System in SAP Integration Suite, Starter Edition?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently using SAP Integration Suite, Starter Edition, with Cloud Integration capacity.

As part of our integration project, we need to implement a queuing mechanism to manage a high volume of incoming requests and process them reliably without overloading the integration flow or the target SAP system.

We would like your recommendations regarding the following points:

  • Is it possible to implement a queuing system with the Starter Edition?
  • Does this edition support JMS queues, or is an additional capability or license required?
  • If JMS is unavailable, what alternative architecture would you recommend?
  • Can Data Store operations be used to create a reliable queuing mechanism?
  • How can we control parallel processing, retries, message order, and error handling?
  • Are there any known capacity or performance limitations in the Starter Edition?

Our objective is to receive requests from an external system, store them temporarily, and process them asynchronously according to the available capacity.

Any recommended architecture, configuration example, or relevant documentation would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
Here is my submission:


Step 1)  schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

type TurnStatus : String enum {
    Waiting;
    Playing;
    Moving;
    Blocked;
    Finished;
}

entity BoardSquares {
    key square : Integer;
    snakeTo : Integer;
    ladderTo : Integer;
    isDoubleHead : Boolean default false;
}

entity GameSessions {
    key ID : UUID;
    name : String(100);
    createdAt : Timestamp;
    startedAt : Timestamp;
    finishedAt : Timestamp;
    winner : Association to Players;
}

@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
    key ID : UUID;
    session : Association to GameSessions;
    name : String(100);
    @readonly
    position : Integer default 1;
    @readonly
    TurnStatus : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
    turnOrder : Integer;
    lastRoll : Integer;
}

Step 2)  game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {
    entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

    @flow.status: TurnStatus
    entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions{

        @from : [ #Waiting ]
        @TO : #Playing
        action startTurn();

        @from:  [ #Playing ]
        @TO : #Moving
        action rollDice() returns Integer;

        @from : [ #Moving ]
        @TO : #Waiting 
        action confirmMove();

        @from : [ #Moving ]
        @TO : #Blocked
        action blockPlayer();

        @from : [ #Blocked ]
        @TO : $flow.previous
        action unblockPlayer();

        @from : [ #Moving ]
        @TO : #Finished
        action winGame();
    }
    entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 Step 3) Output:

Step 4)  I kept TurnStatus as is.

entry.title: Intracompany - How to transfer stock from EWM to non-EWM plant?
entry.content:
Hi Support,

Our customer has a process of moving stocks from a warehouse managed plant to non warehouse managed plant. No delivery cost involved. 

They're currently in private cloud and how they do it is through simple transfer posting mvt 301 - where it generated transfer orders afterwards. But I believe its different now in public cloud.

Can you please help me on determining which is the best scope ID that fits his process?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Intracompany - How to transfer stock from EWM to non-...
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Based on the process described, the recommended SAP Best Practice for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is Scope Item 2VL – SAP EWM Integration: Stock Transport Orders.

Since Plant 1004 is EWM-managed and Plant 1005 is not EWM-managed, the stock transfer should be executed using a Stock Transport Order (STO). The outbound delivery is replicated to SAP EWM, where the warehouse execution is performed through Warehouse Tasks. After the Goods Issue is posted in EWM, the receiving plant, which is not EWM-managed, completes the process by posting the Goods Receipt in Inventory Management (IM).

This differs from the process in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud or SAP ERP with classic Warehouse Management, where a simple transfer posting using movement type 301 could generate WM Transfer Orders. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, warehouse execution is based on the Stock Transport Order process integrated with EWM, and warehouse activities are performed using Warehouse Tasks instead of classic WM Transfer Orders.

Please note that the fact that no delivery costs are involved does not affect the recommended process. Delivery costs are optional within a Stock Transport Order and are not the determining factor when selecting the applicable scope item.

Relevant SAP Documentation

  • SAP Learning – Carrying out Internal Warehouse Processes (3BU, 4MM, 3BW, 4A2, 3BX, 2VN, 2VL, 2YL)
    Explains the complete business process, supported scenarios (including transfers from EWM-managed to non-EWM-managed storage locations), and the end-to-end process flow.
  • SAP Signavio Process Navigator – Scope Item 2VL: SAP EWM Integration – Stock Transport Orders
    Contains the SAP Best Practices process flow, scope description, test scripts, and setup information for Scope Item 2VL.
  • Basic Integration with SAP Extended Warehouse Management (SAP EWM) | SAP Help Portal

Please let us know if you have further questions, we keep at your service!

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: First steps to work with SAP Cloud ALM Deployment scenario for SAP ABAP systems (7.40 or higher)
entry.content:
Hello @DoloresCorrea 

 

Question regarding the "Identical SIDs are not supported" restriction

Thanks for the detailed write-up. I'm trying to reconcile this restriction with what is probably the most common real-world migration scenario today: an SAP ECC 6.0 system being converted to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (RISE with SAP).

During an ECC-to-S/4HANA conversion using DMO (DMOVE2S4 / DMO with System Move), the source ECC system and the resulting S/4HANA system intentionally retain the same SID. This isn't an edge case.... but ....it's the standard SAP-recommended migration approach for the vast majority of customers.

Personally, I've been involved in dozens of ECC-to-S/4HANA Private Cloud migrations, and I honestly can't recall a single project where the SID was changed during the conversion.

This is why I'm struggling to understand the statement that identical SIDs are not supported for Cloud ALM Retrofit.

In a typical RISE conversion project, the maintenance track (ECC) and the implementation track (S/4HANA) are essentially the same system at different points in its lifecycle, sharing the same SID throughout the project.

Could you help clarify the following?

  • Does this mean that Cloud ALM Retrofit cannot be configured if the on-premise ECC development system and the SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud development system have the same SID?

  • If so, is there a supported workaround for RISE customers who desperatly need Retrofit during an S/4HANA conversion project?

If my understanding is correct 😢, it would appear that Cloud ALM Retrofit is not usable for the majority of ECC customers following SAP's standard migration path to S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. Given how strongly SAP is encouraging customers to move to RISE, that seems like a significant functional gap.

entry.title: Re: Media Handling in SAP CAP: Mixing Static and Uploaded Images in Fiori Elements V4
entry.content:
@Shubham_kumar_sap 

Great write-up, thanks for sharing the deep-dive into the Fiori V4 optimization trap!

One thing worth mentioning: for the "fetch hidden columns" part specifically, you can avoid the custom after('READ') interceptor by using the PresentationVariant/RequestAtLeast annotation, which tells Fiori V4 to always include those fields in $select even when they're not shown as columns:

annotate CatalogService.Image @(
  com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.PresentationVariant : {
    Visualizations : [ '@UI.LineItem' ],
    RequestAtLeast : [ mediaType, ImageUrl ]
  }
);

https://ui5.sap.com/#/topic/82e27ffdc7804561970e9e84e2b4a4ef

entry.title: SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation
entry.content:
This is the first blog post in a series introducing the topic of AI in SAP Activate. Follow this blog as we will update it with links to other posts as they are released.

In this blog post, I want to share how SAP Activate is evolving with SAP Business AI and what is already available to you today to leverage in your implementation projects. As SAP expands the availability of AI capabilities and more assistants and agents become available for our customers, SAP Activate is keeping pace to ensure you have the guidance you need to adopt, extend, and apply these capabilities effectively during your journey to the Autonomous Suite.

When we think about Business AI in the context of SAP Activate, we organize it into three interconnected dimensions. Each dimension addresses a different aspect of how AI applies in the implementation lifecycle. In the rest of this post, I will walk you through each of these dimensions, explain what is available today, and highlight how you can start benefiting from these capabilities right away.

Three Dimensions of Business AI in SAP Activate

Before I get into the details, let me introduce the framework. The three dimensions are:

  1. Adopting Business AI capabilities and SAP Joule by SAP customers during their implementation and beyond.
  2. Extending SAP solutions with AI capabilities and agents built on the SAP Business AI Platform.
  3. Leveraging AI capabilities for implementation activities.

These dimensions are interconnected but address different audiences and project stages. Let me expand on each one.

1. Adopting SAP Business AI and SAP Joule

The first dimension is about guiding you, the customer, to activate, set up, and adopt SAP Joule and SAP Business AI capabilities as part of your implementation. In other words, this is about integrating AI into how your organization runs its business processes in the SAP Autonomous Suite.

Think of it this way: when your project team starts scoping the implementation and later runs fit-to-standard workshops, they should be considering not only the standard business processes but also the AI capabilities that come with them. Which processes benefit from AI-powered automation or recommendations? Where does SAP Joule provide value to your end users in their day-to-day work? What agents and assistants would enrich the business processes in our organization? These are the questions SAP Activate helps you answer.

Already in 2025, SAP Activate was enhanced with guidance to help customers activate, set up, and adopt SAP Joule and available SAP Business AI capabilities (see the Business AI catalog on SAP Discovery Center). The guidance in SAP Activate includes self-enablement tasks, guidance for running fit-to-standard workshops with AI considerations, and procedures for setting up the necessary integrations. As an example, the information for the setup of SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is available here (the tasks are tagged with Joule tag).

How do you find this content? You can select the SAP Business AI and SAP Joule tags in the road map viewer in SAP for Me to identify the relevant tasks for your implementation scenario. The flow is straightforward: identify the available SAP Business AI capabilities and SAP Joule features, assess and validate them during fit-to-standard, activate and set up during Realize, and enable your business users to adopt the new capability.

Example of a task for the initial setup of Joule in SAP Cloud ERP Private

We plan to continue expanding this coverage throughout 2026, with increased focus on the new AI capabilities and agents as they become available to customers, and to incorporate steps to consider and embed these capabilities into the scope of your implementation.

Dimension 2: Extending with SAP Business AI Platform Services

The second dimension is for organizations that need to go beyond the pre-built AI capabilities. It supports customers in leveraging the SAP Business AI Platform to build custom AI solutions and agents that extend standard SAP application capabilities in line with the clean core strategy.

This is an important distinction: the first dimension is about adopting what SAP delivers out of the box. The second dimension is about building your own AI-powered extensions on the platform when your business requires something unique. The key principle here is that these extensions follow the clean core approach, ensuring your solution remains upgradeable and maintainable.

SAP Activate for SAP Business Technology Platform already covers various AI-related topics, including guidance for application development on SAP BTP and implementation guidance for AI capabilities. The use of platform services to extend solution guidance is also embedded in the implementation roadmaps for our ERP solutions in the Extensibility work stream, for both private and public cloud solutions. You can access it here.

The flow in your project follows the extensibility work stream: identify and confirm extensibility requirements during fit-to-standard workshops (or earlier if the requirements are already known), leverage SAP Business AI Platform services to extend the standard solution and test during the Realize phase, and finally enable your business users to adopt the new capability.

We plan to continue evolving this implementation guidance as new platform capabilities and services become available, including the new agents and assistants announced at SAPPHIRE 2026.

Dimension 3: Leveraging AI Capabilities for Implementation

The third dimension is where I expect many of you reading this post will find the most immediate value. This is about applying AI capabilities throughout your implementation project to accelerate requirements gathering, design, development, testing, and knowledge transfer across the project lifecycle.

This dimension covers a broad range of AI capabilities like Joule on road map viewer, SAP Joule for Consultants, and embedded AI capabilities in the integrated toolchain, including SAP LeanIX, SAP Signavio, SAP Cloud ALM, and WalkMe.

AI-Assisted Tasks

Earlier in June, SAP Activate released the first wave of AI-assisted implementation tasks. This initial release included 9 updated SAP Activate tasks, 13 new tested prompts, and updated accelerators, focused on SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

These AI-assisted tasks are designed to support your project team in leveraging AI capabilities during implementation. Rather than focusing on information retrieval, we focused on high-impact tasks and deliverables where AI can make a real difference. SAP Activate delivers tested prompts and example outputs, and in some cases, updated accelerators.

The initial focus is on Joule for Consultants, SAP Cloud ALM, and SAP Test Automation Tool through all project stages, covering seven front-runner topics:

  1. Self-enablement on Joule for Consultants – helping your team get started with the capability.
  2. Project execution and artifacts preparation – accelerating the creation of project management deliverables.
  3. Preparation for fit-to-standard workshops – using AI to help consultants prepare effectively for these critical sessions.
  4. Generating requirements from fit-to-standard workshop artifacts – processing workshop results into requirements and user stories in SAP Cloud ALM with assistance from Joule for Consultants.
  5. Generating design documents for extensions – using AI to draft extension design documentation.
  6. Preparing for data loads  – leveraging AI to help with data migration strategy and approach.
  7. Testing – preparing testing strategy and approach, as well as automating preparation of test scripts, determination of test scope, and management of testing.

An AI-assisted task includes two key elements: an AI-assisted procedure section in the task description that explains how to use the AI capability, and a link to the prompt library. The prompt library provides access to the prompt text, example output, and additional details for prompting the AI tool. We built a centralized prompt library to maintain the integrity of prompts linked to SAP Activate and to ensure all users can access them, even if they do not yet have direct access to the AI capability where a dedicated prompt library may exist.

You can find these tasks by filtering for the AI-Assisted Procedure tag in the SAP Activate roadmap viewer or SAP Cloud ALM when accessing the Task Management app. The SAP Activate team will continue to expand the coverage with AI-assisted tasks in the future. Let us know your feedback on the current tasks and your suggestions for areas to enhance.

Joule in SAP Activate Road Map Viewer on SAP for Me

I also want to highlight a capability that has been available since June 2025 and continues to bring great value to our users. SAP Joule is embedded directly in the SAP Activate road map viewer on SAP for Me. This is a conversational AI assistant that enables you to surface the guidance you need through natural-language questions, eliminating the need to manually navigate the roadmap structures.

You can ask Joule your SAP Activate-related questions, carry out a conversation, and copy the answers to share with your partners or colleagues. For example, you might ask about the sequence of activities for data migration, or about which accelerators are available for a specific topic. Joule will provide a clear response based on the SAP Activate content.

To try it, navigate to the SAP Activate road map viewer and click on the Joule icon in the top right corner. You can read more about this capability in the blog post from Chandan: Say Hi to Joule in SAP Activate road map viewer.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we will continue enriching SAP Activate with AI guidance for implementation experts – project managers, consultants, developers, and architects – on how to use available AI capabilities during projects.

In the mid-to-long term, we will evolve SAP Activate as new agents and assistants become available in SAP Cloud ALM, aligned with the agentic AI transformation platform vision shared at SAPPHIRE 2026. We anticipate significant evolution as new agents are built and integrated into the offerings through 2026.

I encourage you to explore the AI-assisted tasks in the road map viewer, try Joule on SAP for Me, and share your experience. Please use the comments to this blog post to continue the conversation or reach out to us at sapactivatemethodology@sap.com with your feedback and suggestions. We are excited to hear how you are leveraging these capabilities in your projects.

Other blogs in this series

To deep-dive into AI-assisted tasks and capabilities, access the blogs linked below (we will add the links as the blogs get published).

 

Useful Resources

  • SAP Activate road map viewer
  • Introducing Joule
  • Setting Up Joule Across Your Organizational System Landscapes
  • Introducing SAP Joule for Consultants
  • Implementing Joule Across Your Organizational System Landscape

entry.title: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Integration Suite provides a unified environment for connecting applications, data, and business processes across cloud and on-premise landscapes. As organizations increasingly expose business capabilities through APIs, APIs are no longer just interfaces to backend systems—they have become a fundamental building block for enterprise integration.

API-centric integration brings API design and integration capabilities together in a unified experience. It enables developers to create APIs that directly connect to backend systems, mediate and transform requests and responses, compose multiple services, and expose simplified interfaces to API consumers. With this approach, developers can build APIs ranging from simple managed endpoints to rich integration scenarios while applying security, traffic management, and lifecycle governance as part of the same development experience.

This blog is the first part of a two-part series that walks through the complete API lifecycle on the Integration Cell runtime.

In Part 1, you will activate the required capabilities and the Integration Cell runtime, create your first API Artifact, explore its design and runtime components, deploy it, and verify the deployment using the monitoring console.

In Part 2, we will explore Developer Hub, the centralized catalog in SAP Integration Suite for publishing and discovering APIs, Events, and MCP Servers. You will learn how to bundle the deployed API into an API Product, publish it for discovery, create and subscribe a developer application, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the published API.

Across both blogs, you will complete the following end-to-end journey:

Activate Integration Cell → Design API → Deploy → Publish → Subscribe → Consume

Understanding the API-Centric Integration

Traditional API management often starts with an existing backend service and exposes it through a managed API endpoint. API-centric integration extends this model by allowing integration capabilities to be designed directly as part of the API.

An API can therefore do more than simply proxy requests to a backend system. Depending on the business scenario, it can connect to backend services, transform and mediate messages, combine data from multiple sources, and expose a simplified interface tailored to the needs of API consumers.

API-centric integration supports scenarios such as:

  • API proxying — expose an existing backend service through a secure and managed API endpoint.
  • API mediation — transform or enrich requests and responses between API consumers and backend systems.
  • API simplification — hide backend complexity and expose a simpler, consumer-friendly API contract.

These capabilities are brought together through a unified API modelling experience. Developers can design the API contract and configure the required integration logic as part of the same API Artifact, then deploy it to the Integration Cell runtime.

For a simple API, the runtime flow can be represented as:

API Consumer → API Artifact → Backend Service

For more advanced scenarios, the API Artifact can contain the integration logic required to transform, mediate, or compose backend services before returning a response to the consumer. In the following sections, we will start with the simplest API-centric integration scenario: creating an API Artifact that exposes a backend service through a managed API endpoint on Integration Cell.

 

Prerequisites

Getting started with API Management on Integration Cell involves enabling the runtime and using the unified design environment to create, deploy, and manage API Artifacts.

Before proceeding, ensure that:

  • Your SAP Integration Suite tenant is configured and the API Management capability is activated.
  • Integration Cell runtime activation
  • You have the required role collections assigned for provisioning, designing and subscription across Integration Suite and Developer Hub applications.

With these prerequisites in place, the end-to-end journey involves four main steps:

  1. Enable API Management and configure access to Developer Hub.
  2. Activate Integration Cell from the tenant settings.
  3. Design and deploy APIs using the unified modelling experience.
  4. Create an application in Developer Hub, subscribe to the API product, and consume the API.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

Before you can design and deploy API Artifacts to Integration Cell, you need to activate the API Management capability and activate the Integration Cell runtime. Make sure you have Integration_Provisioner role collection assigned.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Home → Manage Capabilities

In the Select Capabilities step, select Manage APIs and choose Next.

In the API Management configuration step:

  • API Modelling is selected by default.
  • Select Developer Hub to enable API discovery and consumption.

Choose Next.

Review the selected capabilities in the Summary step and choose Activate.

 

Once the activation is complete, API Modelling is available for designing API Artifacts, and Developer Hub can be used for API discovery and consumption.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

After enabling the API Management capability, navigate to runtime management.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

The Integration Cell tab displays the current status of the runtime. If the runtime has not yet been activated, its status is shown as Inactive. Choose Activate to start provisioning the Integration Cell runtime.

Integration Cell is a fully managed runtime, so no Kubernetes cluster or infrastructure configuration is required from the user. The underlying runtime environment is provisioned and managed by SAP.

Verify the Runtime

After activation is complete, return to:

SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

Verify that the Integration Cell status is shown as Active.

The runtime is now ready for API Artifact deployment.

Before proceeding to API modelling, assign the PI_Integration_Developer role collection. This role collection provides the required access to design and deploy API Artifacts to Integration Cell.

Artifacts Supported on Integration Cell
SAP Integration Suite enables you to design and manage two types of artifacts for deployment to Integration Cell: 

API enable you to expose backend services through managed API endpoints. They provide capabilities for securing APIs, managing traffic, transforming and mediating requests and responses, and governing APIs throughout their lifecycle.

MCP server enable you to expose enterprise capabilities and data through the Model Context Protocol (MCP), making them accessible to AI-powered applications and agents.

In this article, we will focus on the API Artifact and walk through the process of creating, deploying, publishing, and consuming it on Integration Cell.

Create Your First API Artifact

With the Integration Cell runtime activated and the content package in place, you are ready to create your first API Artifact. API Artifacts are created within a content package, allowing related APIs and integration assets to be organized and managed together.

Navigate to the  content package in this case Product and Inventory Services and choose Artifacts > Add > API.

Step 1: Select the Runtime Profile

The first step is to choose the runtime profile where the API Artifact will be deployed. The selected runtime profile determines the capabilities available for the API Artifact.

Select Integration Cell as the runtime profile and choose Next.

 

Step 2: Select the API Creation Method

The next step is to choose how you want to create the API Artifact. Integration Cell supports multiple creation methods to address different integration scenarios.

  • URL or Specification – Create an API by providing a backend URL, importing an API specification/metadata in json/yaml/edmx format or uploading an API zip bundle.
  • API Provider – Create an API by discovering services from a backend system or by using a configured destination.
  • Reusable API – Create an API that invokes another API Artifact using the API Direct adapter, enabling modular and reusable API-centric integration scenarios.

For this blog, select URL or Specification and choose Next.

Step 3: Provide the API Details

In the final step, configure the details of the API Artifact. Depending on your use case, you can create the API Artifact in one of two ways:

  • Create – Generate an API Artifact by providing the URL of the backend service. The API definition is discovered from the backend endpoint.
  • Upload – Create an API Artifact by importing an existing API definition in YAML/JSON, EDMX, or an API ZIP bundle.

For this tutorial, we will use the Upload option and import an OpenAPI specification.

The uploaded OpenAPI specification references an existing SAP BTP Destination named S4Hana_Cloud_Edition.

During deployment and runtime, Integration Cell uses this destination to securely connect to the backend system without exposing backend connection details in the API Artifact.

The destination defines the backend host, authentication details, and connectivity configuration required to access the SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Multiple API artifacts can reuse the same destination, enabling centralized management of backend connectivity.

Next, import the OpenAPI specification and verify that the destination referenced in the specification is resolved automatically.

Complete the remaining API details:

  • Target: S4Hana_Cloud_Edition (Destination)
  • Name: S4Hana Purchase Order
  • ID: S4Hana_Purchase_order
  • Service Type: OData
  • API Base Path: /purchaseorders
  • API State: Active

Choose Add to create the API Artifact.

The API artifact is created and opens in the API Designer, where you can review the generated API definition, configure policies, and prepare it for deployment to the Integration Cell runtime. API Designer provides a unified environment for configuring the API definition, runtime flow, target connectivity, and policies.

Multiple API artifacts can reuse the same destination, enabling centralized management of backend connectivity.

Explore the API Artifact

The API Artifact has now been created and is ready for review before deployment. The artifact provides a consolidated view of the API configuration, backend connectivity, exposed resources, and request processing flow. Understanding these components helps you validate the API and determine where additional configuration, such as policies or transformations, can be applied.

Overview

The Overview tab provides the general information about the API Artifact, including its name, identifier, version, runtime profile, API base path, lifecycle state, and deployment status. It also displays the generated API URL and metadata such as creation and modification details.

This page serves as the central summary of the API Artifact and is useful for verifying its overall configuration.

 

Target Endpoint

The Target Endpoint tab defines where the API forwards incoming requests.

Since the API was created by importing an OpenAPI specification that references an SAP BTP Destination, the artifact automatically resolves the configured destination (S4Hana_Cloud_Edition) along with the relative service path. During runtime, Integration Cell combines the destination URL with the relative path to invoke the backend service.

Using destinations externalizes backend connectivity and credentials from the API Artifact, making the API easier to transport across landscapes while keeping connection details centrally managed.

 

Resources

The Resources tab lists all resources and operations that are exposed by the API Artifact. These resources are generated from the imported API definition and include the supported HTTP methods and corresponding resource paths.

For OData services, the resources represent the exposed entity sets and operations defined by the backend service.

Selecting Open Editor launches the visual API editor, where you can modify resources, update the API definition, add or remove operations, and configure the request processing flow.

Policies

When an API Artifact is created with the specific service type, SAP Integration Suite automatically applies a default processing template. This template provides the runtime flow required to receive, authenticate, authorize, and forward API requests to the backend service.

The default processing flow consists of the following components:

  • OData V2 Sender Adapter
  • Authentication Policy
  • Authorization Policy
  • OData Receiver Adapter

 

OData V2 Sender Adapter

The OData V2 Sender Adapter acts as the entry point for the API Artifact. It exposes the managed OData endpoint that API consumers invoke and forwards incoming requests to the policy model, where the configured security and governance policies are executed before the request is routed to the backend service.

Authentication Policy

The Authentication policy is the first policy executed in the processing flow and is mandatory for every API Artifact. It verifies the identity of the API consumer before allowing the request to proceed. The policy supports multiple authentication mechanisms, including Basic Authentication, OAuth 2.0, and Client Certificate authentication. For newly created API Artifacts, it also supports External OAuth (OIDC), enabling token validation from external identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, Google, and Okta. Multiple authentication methods can be enabled simultaneously, allowing APIs to support different client applications and security requirements.

 

Authorization Policy

After a request is successfully authenticated, the Authorization policy determines whether the authenticated caller is permitted to invoke the API. It evaluates access based on OAuth Scopes, Developer Keys, or a combination of both, providing flexible authorization for different API consumption scenarios.

The policy supports the following authorization modes:

  • OAuth Scope or Developer Key (Default) – Access is granted if the caller presents either the required OAuth scope or a valid Developer Key.
  • OAuth Scope and Developer Key – Both a valid OAuth scope and a Developer Key are required.
  • OAuth Scope Only – Access is granted only if the access token contains the configured OAuth scope.
  • Developer Key Only – Access is granted only to subscribed applications presenting a valid Developer Key.

For OAuth-based authorization, the policy validates the configured Scope Key (JWT claim) and the required Scope value. By default, the scope is set to API.invoke, ensuring that only callers with the appropriate permission can invoke the API.

OData Receiver Adapter

Once all configured policies have been successfully executed, the OData Receiver Adapter forwards the request to the backend OData service using the configured destination. It manages the backend connectivity and returns the response through the same processing pipeline to the API consumer.

Extending the Policy Flow

Beyond the default template, SAP Integration Suite provides a rich set of policies that can be added to the processing flow to address different API management requirements. Common policy categories include:

  • Security – Authenticate and authorize API consumers, validate requests, and protect APIs against malicious or malformed traffic.
  • Traffic Management – Apply policies such as Quota and Spike Arrest to control API consumption and protect backend systems.
  • Transformation and Mediation – Modify request and response payloads, convert data formats, enrich messages, invoke external services, or execute JavaScript and Python scripts as part of the API flow.

For this blog, we will keep the default policy configuration unchanged and proceed with deploying the API Artifact to the Integration Cell runtime. Subsequent blogs in this series will explore how to configure and use these policies to build secure, resilient, and production-ready APIs.

Deploy the API Artifact

After reviewing the API Artifact and its configuration, the next step is to deploy it to the Integration Cell runtime. Deployment publishes the API Artifact to the selected runtime, making it available for invocation through the configured API endpoint.

To deploy the API, click the Deploy icon from the API Designer toolbar. In the deployment dialog, verify that Integration Cell is selected as the Runtime Profile, choose the appropriate Virtual Host, and click Yes to start the deployment.

 

Once the deployment is complete, the API status changes from Not Deployed to Deployed, and the generated API endpoint becomes active on the selected Virtual Host. You can now invoke the API directly or publish it through an API Product for controlled discovery and consumption using Developer Hub.

For any subsequent changes to the API Artifact, save the changes and redeploy the artifact to make the updated configuration effective on the Integration Cell runtime.

 

 

Monitor the Deployed API Artifact

Once the API Artifact has been successfully deployed, you can monitor its runtime status and operational details from the Monitor view in SAP Integration Suite.

Navigation:
Monitor → Integrations and APIs

The Overview page provides a consolidated view of all integration content deployed to the selected runtime profile. It displays runtime health, message processing statistics, deployment status, and operational metrics, allowing you to quickly assess the overall state of your Integration Cell runtime.

To view details of a specific deployment, select the API Artifact from the Manage Integration Content section.


The details page displays comprehensive runtime information about the deployed artifact, including:

  • Deployment Status – Indicates whether the artifact is successfully deployed and running.
  • Endpoints – Displays the runtime endpoint through which the API can be invoked.
  • Artifact Details – Provides deployment metadata and quick navigation to the Artifact Editor or message monitoring.
  • Status Details – Shows the current deployment state and any deployment-related messages.
  • Download and Undeploy – Allows you to download the deployed artifact or remove it from the runtime.

This view serves as the primary operational console for managing deployed API Artifacts on the Integration Cell runtime.

 Log Level

For troubleshooting and diagnostics, the runtime log level of an API Artifact can be configured directly from the monitoring view.

Supported log levels include:

  • None – Disables runtime logging.
  • Info – Records high-level execution events and is recommended for normal operation.
  • Error – Logs only error conditions and exceptions.
  • Debug – Captures detailed execution information for in-depth troubleshooting.

The selected log level controls the amount of diagnostic information captured during API execution and can be adjusted as needed without modifying or redeploying the API Artifact. This capability simplifies production support and helps isolate runtime issues while minimizing operational overhead.

Conclusion (End of Part 1)

In this blog, you activated the Integration Cell runtime, created your first API Artifact, explored its design and runtime components, deployed it successfully, and verified its deployment using the monitoring console.

At this stage, the API is available on the Integration Cell runtime but is not yet discoverable by application developers. The next step is to govern its consumption by publishing it through Developer Hub.

In the next blog, we shift our focus from API design and deployment to API governance and controlled consumption through Developer Hub. We will explore how Developer Hub acts as the centralized catalog for APIs, Events, and MCP Servers. You will learn how to create an API Product, publish it for discovery, create and subscribe a developer application, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the published API. By the end of the second blog, you will have completed the complete API lifecycle—from design and deployment to governed publication and secure consumption.
